solution_uniqueness_guard.reason: Tests assert on the exact comment string 'Initial arrow created on pointer down needs to not update the points' (test_updateBoundPoint_initial_arrow_check_exists), exact regex pattern for pointsEqual call structure (test_updateBoundPoint_returns_null_for_initial_arrow), and specific import source '@excalidraw/math' (test_pointsEqual_function_imported). tests_verify_behavior_not_text.reason: The three fail-to-pass tests (test_updateBoundPoint_initial_arrow_check_exists, test_updateBoundPoint_returns_null_for_initial_arrow, test_pointsEqual_function_imported) all exclusively use `open(path).read()` and string/regex matching. They never import, call, or execute the code. The pass-to-pass tests do run subprocesses, but the core fail-to-pass tests are purely textual.

test_not_tautological.reason: All three fail-to-pass tests only check for specific strings/patterns in the source file. An agent could pass them by simply inserting the exact comment string and code patterns without understanding the bug — e.g., adding dead code with the matching text anywhere in the file.

anti_cheating_measures.reason: The solution is in /workspace/excalidraw's container at solution/solve.sh. The agent could read it directly. Additionally, since the tests only check for exact string patterns, the agent could grep the test file for the expected strings and insert them verbatim.

behavior_in_tests.reason: instruction.md states the arrow should not jump when dragging from within a bindable element, but no test actually verifies this runtime behavior. The fail-to-pass tests only check source code text patterns, not actual arrow drag behavior.

informative_test_structure.reason: Tests are well-named (test_updateBoundPoint_initial_arrow_check_exists, etc.), have descriptive docstrings explaining fail-to-pass vs pass-to-pass, and are logically grouped by purpose.
